rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical Cu-catalyzed 1,2-diazidation of alkenes with TMSN3, probed here by radical-clock (vinylcyclopropane ring-opening, 1,6-diene cyclization) and carbocation-trapping experiments.
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation regenerates Cu(II)/Cu(III)-N3, which delivers an azidyl radical to the alkene; the resulting carbon radical is further oxidized to a carbocation (or trapped by a second Cu-N3) to install the second azide.

key_intermediates.name: β-azido carbon radical from N3 addition to alkene
ring-opened homoallyl/allyl carbon radical from vinylcyclopropane (radical clock)
cyclized pyrrolidinyl carbon radical from 1,6-diene (radical clock)
β-azido tertiary carbocation (from methallyl benzamide substrate)

electron_flow: Cu-azide delivers an N3 radical to the alkene to give a β-azido C-radical; for substrates that can stabilize a cation, this radical is further oxidized (anodically or by Cu(III)) to a carbocation that is trapped by azide, while less-stabilized radicals couple directly with a second Cu-N3.

probe_reagents.name: (1-phenyl-2-vinylcyclopropane) 41
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened 1,5-diazidopent-3-en-1-ylbenzene (42) isolated in 15% (E/Z 5:1), confirming a free carbon radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: N-allyl-N-(2-methylallyl)tosylamide 44
probe_reagents.observation: 5-exo-trig cyclization gives 3,4-bis(azidomethyl)-3-methyl-1-tosylpyrrolidine (45) in 21%, dr 2:1, supporting a radical cyclization prior to second azidation.

probe_reagents.name: N-(2-methylallyl)benzamide 47
probe_reagents.observation: Affords N-(2,3-diazido-2-methylpropyl)benzamide (48) in 59% without amide-cyclization product, consistent with a tertiary β-azido carbocation rapidly trapped by azide rather than by the pendant amide.

notes: Pages shown are SI mechanistic probes (sections b 'Radical clock experiments' and c 'Investigation of carbocation intermediates'). Both a radical and a carbocation pathway are operative: most substrates proceed through radical-polar crossover, while non-stabilized radicals likely undergo direct Cu-mediated azidation without going through a cation.
